speaker: interviewer
text: The date is June 21, 1995. Was there something you'd like to add to what you've been speaking about already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I would like to add this that-- excuse me-- just came to me, although I've thought about it at other times. I had one particular-- particularly good friend who was non-Jewish. Her name was Gerta Harnish, and-- a very smart girl, whom I became acquainted with and friends with in my first four years in grade school. And then she went on to the same high school that I did, where I was-- where things were so bad for me. But she was not in my class, as I mentioned. No one I knew from the other school was in my class. Her mother came over to our apartment. And my mother and her mother knew each other. We, we had gone tobogganing when we were much younger. We knew each other. And she came over, even though you were not supposed to go. That was already-- at the time, you were not supposed to go to Jewish homes or apartments or have anything to do with Jews. And she wanted to explain why, why her Gerta could no longer have anything to do with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anything that she said, of course, sounded just plain awful to my mother, you know. And she was sick about the whole thing, about hearing it out of this woman's mouth. How can you talk that way, you know, when you know we-- you know we're good people? We-- you know, we're not guilty of any of those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but she came over. And she wanted to explain that Gerta just could not be my friend anymore. And it, it-- it's just another one of those sad, little things that piled up and ended up so horrible. Another thing that I have to-- that I was thinking of right now that is amazing, that when you heard all these things said about Jews, and at every corner then, what had been done. At just about every street corner, they had was a, a scandal sheet by Julius Streicher, one of the most outrageous, vicious Nazis-- Julius Streicher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-- Julius and Streicher, S-T-R-E-I-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other word?

speaker: interviewee
text: the, the publication-- so-called. S-T-U umlaut R-M-E-R. This was in a-- not a kiosk-type setting, but it was-- it was enclosed for people to, to see it and to read the headlines. And I think every headline that they ever had-- and they changed it at least weekly-- showed a, you know, the, the, the, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a sketch of what you'd call the ugliest Jew-- what Arafat looks like, as a matter of fact. I shouldn't say that. But, you know, he looks like what they used to picture as a very ugly Jew. He Is sort of the personification, you know, the terrible nose, and the-- and whatever, you know. There's half, half, half-laugh, or whatever it is, on the face. And then the worst stories about Jews, you know, what kind of rats they are, vermin, and whatever-- the worst kind of things. And that was at every-- just about every-- at many street corners. The other thing that you had, every weekend, the Nazis would collect money. So the SA, the brown shirts, would be out in the street. And they would be jangling there-- like the good Salvation Army does-- they would be jangling their cans, you know, for people to put money in there. And they saw someone who would pass them up and not give them money, they would immediately know who that was because they were people from the various neighborhoods. Then you heard Hitler's, Hitler's voice blasting by loudspeaker at least once a week on the streets. All of-- all of those kinds of things, that's how, you know, things went on after they took over the government. Almost immediately, they started flying airplanes. So within, within a few months, you know, they planned on making war later-- within a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the SA stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SA was-- those were really the lower-- the lower-class Nazis that actually stood-- it stood for 'Sturmabteilung,' as far as I, I can remember. Whereas the SS stood for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Schutzstaffel,' the elite guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're a child, you're-- right now, how old are you? This is '33, '34.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, '33 through '39, during their regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are how old during these years again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm from 9, 9 through 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about what that was like for you as a child, walking through those streets, what it felt at home, what was going on in your house, and how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first, a lot of Jews, and even a lot of Germans, who had seen this thing come about, of them being empowered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thought it couldn't last, that it was just too ridiculous to last, that their plans were outrageous that the former, you know, so-called allies-- or like France, France and England, would never stand for it. But as it was, they were sick and tired of having fought the First World War. And they were still feeling the terrible effects of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember in your house, was that the feeling? Or was there a lot of fear in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it would not last?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --feeling in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my father thought maybe it might not last. My mother thought it would last and get worse. My mother, always, from the very beginning, had that feeling, and correctly so. And she started pushing my father to write-- my father had three sisters in the United States. One of them was extremely well set and, and in very good financial circumstances, I should say. And-- excuse me-- and she urged my father to start writing to, to him-- to her-- excuse-- to him, her husband, Oscar Wolf and Minna Wolf. And-- excuse me-- I don't know if he actually did right away. He may have, like, put it in maybe sort of veiled language in the beginning. My mother was adamant about the fact, that it was her intuition that this was going to last, that it was going to get worse, and that the only-- the only hope we would have is by getting out of the country. Now, there are other people who left right away, in '33, but very few--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have family that left in '33 and '34?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, did not. Anyway, excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- as the years went on, and as the laws that the Nazis passed became worse and worse, and after, after the Kristallnacht, you just knew you had to get out. Everybody knew that they had to get out. But everybody could not get out. There was no country that opened its doors like the United States did when Russia invaded Hungary, if you recall that-- came in with tanks to Budapest. And the United States opened its doors and let the Hungarians in. Nobody opened their doors for the Jews. It, it was a-- an unbelievably pathetic, sad chapter in history. You could come into the-- into the-- into the United States with an affidavit properly executed here, in the United States, by a blood relation who had the financial wherewithals to support you, or your family, or whoever, until such time as you could make your own way, so that nobody should fall in-- fall onto the state and be-- so that the state-- the United States would not be liable for supporting anybody, keeping anybody alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to talk a little bit about Kristallnacht. As a young person, what do you remember about that? And what happened in-- for you and your family shortly after that, in terms of freedoms and how you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, so on that morning, which was the morning of the 10th or 11th of November, 1938, I was walking to school. And we didn't have a telephone. And a lot of people didn't. And the streets were unusually quiet. And there were some women who were walking outside, crying, beside themselves. I, I remember at least two. And it looked like they were still in their nightclothes. Now, normally, you know, in Germany, which is a very formal country, [LAUGHS] you don't see that. And when I arrived at the school, there was, was really next to nobody there. But we were told that there would be no school and that during the night, the government had arrested a lot of Jewish men, that a lot of things had been set on fire, destroyed, and-- the stores, the businesses. And it, it was an attack, supposedly, in response to the shooting of this minor official in Paris by this young, Polish Jew. But it was-- it was, in effect, an ordered response by the SA and the SS. In the smaller towns, as it was throughout the Nazi period, it was always much worse because everybody knew who you were. In Hamburg, a big city, I mean, other than your neighbors and friends, nobody else knew you were Jewish, unless you very definitely looked typically Jewish. So-- but they know who the Jewish-- who the Jewish store owners were. So they had smashed all the plate glass, and-- which I saw the next day. I went home. And nothing had happened in our apartment building. And my father was at home. And I, I can't say when did we find out what. My father was home. My mother was home. And they said that I should stay home. But the next few days, my father needed for me to, to do an errand to downtown, which wasn't very far from us, and which I was very familiar with. And that's when I saw-- Hamburg has a lot of canals. I saw the mannequins from the fashion stores and the department stores floating in the canals. And the-- everything was broken. It was a horrible sight, and-- of, of, of stores that belonged to Jewish owners. And then, of course, we found out that my mother's youngest brother had been taken into custody and that my mother's oldest brother in Wattenscheid, Westphalia had been taken into custody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wattenscheid? That's where my mother was born. It's in Westphalia. It's now called Rhine-Westphalia. It's W-A-T-T-E-N-S-C-H-E-I-D. And-- excuse me. I recall several days later, they were still picking up men. I don't know how that came to us or how we found out about that. They were still picking up people. And a couple of days later, when I was at home-- I don't know how many days it was before we went back to school. When I was in-- at home, I heard-- and this was during the day-- I heard some people walking up the stairs in the-- in the-- stairway, out in the apartment-building stairway. And these were boots. You could tell they were boots. The Nazis all had, with their uniforms, these boots that went up to the knee, those solid, stiff boots. And you could hear the boots. And then-- we had-- there was the window. And I could see through the window. And there were two-- there was a widow with two young Jewish sons-- I mean, young; they were maybe 20, 22-- living upstairs of us. And there they had been-- they had been handcuffed and taken into custody. And they were being marched down the stairs. So they had taken these two Jacoby boys. They passed by our apartment. How that happened, I don't know. Because the name 'Wallach' was a Jewish-- it's a Jewish name. It's very definitely a Jewish name. And it was listed on the-- at the entrance. And it's just a mystery. It was just pure luck they did not pick up my father. I thought, for sure, on the way down, they would stop. And he wasn't home, of course. And-- excuse me. I, I took a walk. Whether it was that day-- I, I can't-- I cannot recall which day it was. And I somehow passed by the main synagogue building, which was the synagogue at the Bornstrasse. And again, that's-- they were all named-- the temple was named after this street it was in. The schools were named after the streets that they were on. And the, the synagogue at the Bornstrasse was the main synagogue-- and big, beautiful building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-R-N-S-T-R-A-S-S-E, Bornstrasse. And the building, by that time, had been-- I saw them poking and stoking through where the bimah was. And it had been, I would say, half-demolished. It had been burned. It had been-- they had taken a-- the artifacts out of it. And they were going to destroy the rest of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people had gathered around to stand and look at it because there were still embers from the fire. And, and I was looking to my left. I was looking to my right. And I wanted to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you knew that you can't say anything. You cannot say-- you cannot-- you could not express yourself in any way in Germany after the Nazis came to power because anything-- you were-- you could-- you would be hauled away right away. So you had to keep your mouth shut.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what I wanted-- which, at that time, became very difficult. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You wanted to say, oh, how terrible! What did they do here? What a terrible thing to do! So people were just standing there and looking and saying nothing. And my-- and then the inside of me, my feeling was, I want to be able to get out of here in the worst way and tell the world about this. That was my feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that-- and in that vein, at that point in time, it was my daily prayer before I went to sleep that we would get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a result of that, then what happened in terms of your family and the preparations to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that-- as a result of that-- excuse me-- and I can say for sure now-- that was in November, 1938. We, we got on the boat August 10, 1939, after a waiting period because there were quotas. The American government had quotas for every country, whereby you were assigned a quota number. Unless it was a country like, say, Denmark or Sweden, where there weren't so many people that were dying to get out of there-- but a quota number is what you needed to get from the American government. They needed to see if your-- if your affidavit was in order and correct-- and which they finally had-- my uncle finally had sent. But I think that he had sent it before the Kristallnacht. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- excuse me-- and-- because the waiting period at that time-- because so many more Jews were really trying to get out, the waiting period was about a year. So a year would have taken us to July or August of 1938 that we would have had to present it. I don't recall it. I do recall going to the American Embassy, while you're bringing it up-- not the embassy, the Consulate, and, and undergoing a very undignified physical examination. It was awful. [LAUGHS] I'm not-- I'm not going to go into details of it. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country? Was this in Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg-- the American Consulate. And so, we had this quota number assigned. And it was a wait over here. And my mother was fearful that, in a year's time, things would be so much worse, that everybody would be hauled off. And-- however, that did not come about. And we were lucky that we were still all in one piece by the time it was time for us to book a passage on a-- on a liner to cross the Atlantic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was going to say something else. Now what was it? It got to be-- well, when, when my uncle was released from, from the [GERMAN], they called it-- 'KZ.' The concentration camp was called 'Kah Zed' for short-- K, 'kah,' 'zed.' That's the German letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He talked about-- he wasn't much worse for the experience, except-- because they, they were strong. My mother's brothers were strong people. They were not mollycoddled. They were-- my-- her older brother had been in World War I. This younger brother had not. But this younger brother had-- during the bad years in Germany, where all these parties were fighting for one another and the communists were trying to take over, he had fought what was called the 'Red Army' in Germany, at a certain point in time in the '20s. He was too young to be in the World War I army. But he had actually fought in the streets the communists, as a good German. And because of that-- and he had documentation from the authorities, which his wife presented-- because of that, he got out very soon. He got out of the concentration camp near Hamburg very soon. My mother's other brother in [GERMAN], who was incarcerated in the-- in the concentration camp near Berlin, then first went to his sister there in Berlin, where I had stayed. And he told all the stories about all the horrible things. But he, too, wasn't much worse for the experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They talked about the-- the-- those who were in the worst physical condition, how they would go at those first, knock their heads against the wall until the brains spilled out. I mean, you know, the, the most horrible things you can imagine, which they continued to do throughout, until the war-- it was all over at the end of the Second World War-- the most horrible cruelties, bestialities that they were capable of, that, that, that man could be capable of. And so shortly after that, then, my uncle in-- my uncle in Hamburg, the youngest brother of my mother, made-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were there were ways to get into Australia, if you had a trade that the Australians wanted or a business-- a trade that the Australians wanted. Now he was in the typewriter business. But he was extremely talented as an electrician. He could do any kind of electrical work, had done it since he was a kid. And nobody knows where he picked it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he passed an exam as an electrician. And he became an, an electrical contractor in Australia. And that's how he got out to Australia. Now, the-- And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the spring of 1939. So he got out. The Rothenburgs-- my, my Aunt Paula and Max, and their children, Inge and Hans Joachin, they had no way. I mean, he was-- he was a manufacturer's representative. And he could-- I mean, there was nothing that he could present himself to be or do to get into Australia. Now, there-- same was true of my aunt-- my aunt and uncle in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, there were ways for Jews to get out of Germany. I mean, you wonder, you know, well, did they all stay there by choice? No, they didn't stay there by choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was no place you could get in. You could get into some South-American and Central-American countries by paying large sums of money under the table at the consulate, for which you would not get a receipt. Now a very good-- excuse me. There was Uruguay and Paraguay-- people went there. A very good example of the failure of that was this ship full of Jews who went to Cuba and then were-- and had paid very large sums of money to the Cuban government and then were refused entry. It was either-- it was probably in early 1939 also-- and then eventually, were refused entry here, tried to dock here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family, then-- you've told me about some people who were able to go to Australia. What was going on in your own home and with other relatives in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in our own home, then, we had an affidavit. And we had to wait until our quota number would be called. And so my mother and father and I, you know, we're-- I was still going to school. And my father was, really, almost unable to make any kind of a living anymore. And-- excuse me. My mother had an inheritance that kind of kept us going, that was still coming in. But there was nothing. There was waiting. There was waiting to start a new life, more or less. And less and less, a man-- a person who looked what you could call Jewish or Semitic, you know, could less and less, really show themselves out in the street. There were wanton attacks after the Kristallnacht. And so it was a matter of waiting for the time when we could leave. And in the meantime, my other relatives, the Rothenburgs, had to move out of their-- out of their co-op apartment because it was being cleared of Jews, even though it was their own apartment that they had paid for. And they had to move into another smaller apartment, less desirable, but whatever. And it was at that apartment that we spent our last night. Because our stuff was packed, and our furniture was sold. And we had gotten a letter from the husband of my father's sister, who gave the affidavit, that we were to bring nothing but our clothes, really, with us because there was no room to store it. That turned out to be a big lie because the man had a big business here. When-- that if he couldn't store it in his beautiful, gorgeous apartment on [? Belmont ?] Avenue, then he could have stored it somewhere else. So we, we had-- we came with, really, nothing, with, you know, clothes on our backs and what was in the suitcases. And they allowed us, then, to take two place settings of silver per person. That was the German government's allowance. And so that's-- you know, the time was passed doing that. In the meantime, we had had-- my father, you know, dared to be-- dared not to be seen very much outside anymore. We had always enjoyed a wonderful afternoon at the Elbe River, out in a suburb that was called-- it's called Blankenese, B-L-A-N-K-E-N-E-S-E, where you sit way up on a cliff-like area. And you look down to the river. And you see the ships-- the passenger ships pulling out to the North Sea. It was on the way to the North Sea. The river was very wide there already. You'd hear the band playing. And, you know, people were going to have a wonderful passage going over there. And there was-- there were, you know, cakes and-- excuse me-- petit fours to be bought. And my parents would drink coffee. And it was very nice. You could sit there all afternoon and enjoy the sights. And we no longer went there. Because the last time we had gone there, they-- there was a whole large group of SS people at the-- that restaurant. And, and we sat outdoors. It was just a wonderful thing, wonderful place to go. And then my father had the feeling that they were staring him down. And he no longer wanted to go there. And so all in all, and more and more, Jews were totally disowned. Jews were totally disowned of everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- I don't know if this was just before or right after we had left, my uncle in Wattenscheid, who had inherited the building that my grandfather built-- and the business, had to sold it-- had to sell it to a Gentile, to a butcher down the street, who always had had his eyes on it, for next to nothing. And then-- and then the money was put in an account, which the government overlooked. And they could only withdraw certain small amounts for absolute necessities. And that's how it was with all Jews. And-- the, the, the vice got tighter and tighter. And you knew that, eventually, they would start a war. And it's just-- excuse me-- just a feeling that I had. In fact, before we left in May of 1939, I made a visit to Berlin to see my aunt and uncle once more, who had been so wonderful to me when I was four years old. And they showed me the city. And they showed me, they-- they-- the castle and Potsdam and went all around. I mean, I had been four years old. And I was then 15. And I went-- I went on the train myself to Berlin and back. And when I was on the train, I, I struck up-- some young soldiers struck up a conversation with me, you know, just because I didn't really look Jewish, so nobody knew. What-- they were maybe 17, 18, 19 years old. And so, during the conversation, which is very-- it, it, it-- it's very interesting. During the conversation, they said, well we had been on this and this and these and those kinds of maneuvers. This was in May, '39. And they said, in August, we will probably go--
